---
name: bastion-ssh
description: 'Overview and usage guide for the bastion-ssh project — a portable SSH bastion tool built on Dev Containers. Use when asking about project purpose, setup steps, available make commands, file transfer methods, Cloudflare Tunnel integration, or Ansible provisioning.'
---

# bastion-ssh

A portable SSH bastion tool powered by Dev Containers. Connect to any remote server without modifying your local host environment. Also compatible with GitHub Codespaces.

## Purpose

Isolate SSH credentials and connection logic inside a Dev Container so that:
- No SSH keys or config files are written to the host machine.
- The same workflow runs identically on any machine that supports Dev Containers or Codespaces.

## Repository Structure

```
.devcontainer/       # Dev Container configuration
ansible/
  site.yaml          # Top-level playbook (imports setup.yml)
  ansible.cfg        # Ansible configuration
  inventory/
    hosts.ini        # Auto-generated by setup_ansible.sh — do not edit
    group_vars/
      servers.yaml   # Group variables (ansible_python_interpreter, etc.)
  playbooks/
    setup.yml        # Playbook: install & register cloudflared service
  roles/
    cloudflared/     # Role: download, install, and enable cloudflared
  vault/
    secrets.yml.example  # Ansible Vault template for cf_tunnel_token
tool/
  setup_env.sh       # Interactive .env generator
  setup_ansible.sh   # Load .env and run ansible-playbook
  ssh.sh             # SSH connection wrapper
  rsync.sh           # rsync wrapper with tunnel support
  sftp.sh            # SFTP wrapper with tunnel support
Makefile             # User-facing commands
ansible.cfg          # Root-level Ansible configuration
```

## Configuration

Run the interactive setup to generate `.env`:

```shell
make setup
# Optional: write to a specific path
make setup ARGS=path/to/.env
```

The generated `.env` contains:

| Variable          | Description                                          |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| `HOST`            | Remote server IP address or hostname                 |
| `USER`            | SSH login username                                   |
| `PORT`            | SSH port (default: 22)                               |
| `KEY`             | Path to the private key inside the container         |
| `ENABLE_TUNNEL`   | `true` to route through Cloudflare Tunnel            |
| `SUBDOMAIN`       | Cloudflare Tunnel subdomain (tunnel only)            |
| `DOMAIN`          | Cloudflare Tunnel domain (tunnel only)               |
| `CF_TUNNEL_TOKEN` | Cloudflare Zero Trust Tunnel token (Ansible only)    |
| `SUDO_PASS`       | sudo password for the remote host (Ansible only)     |

> If you get a permission error for the private key, run:
> ```shell
> chmod 600 /root/.ssh/your-key.pem
> ```

## Available Commands

| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `make setup` | Generate `.env` interactively |
| `make setup-ansible` | Generate inventory and run `ansible-playbook` |
| `make ssh` | Open an SSH session to the remote host |
| `make rsync ARGS="..."` | Transfer files via rsync |
| `make sftp ARGS="..."` | Open an interactive SFTP session |

### rsync examples

```shell
# Upload
make rsync ARGS="-av ./local/path/ user@host:/remote/path/"

# Download
make rsync ARGS="-av user@host:/remote/path/ ./local/path/"
```

### setup-ansible examples

```shell
# Apply playbook (prompts for confirmation)
make setup-ansible

# Dry-run (no changes applied)
make setup-ansible ARGS="--check --diff"

# Limit to a specific host
make setup-ansible ARGS="--limit target"

# Use Ansible Vault password file
make setup-ansible ARGS="--vault-password-file /path/to/.vault_pass"
```

## Cloudflare Tunnel (Optional)

When `ENABLE_TUNNEL=true`, all commands (`make ssh`, `make rsync`, `make sftp`, `make setup-ansible`) automatically route through Cloudflare Tunnel. This avoids exposing the SSH port directly to the internet.

### Prerequisites

1. A Cloudflare Zero Trust tunnel has been created.
2. The SSH hostname is registered as a Public Hostname on that tunnel.
3. `cloudflared` is installed inside the Dev Container.

### How it works

- **Without tunnel**: `ssh -i $KEY -p $PORT $USER@$HOST`
- **With tunnel**: `ssh -i $KEY -p $PORT -o ProxyCommand="cloudflared access ssh --hostname $SUBDOMAIN.$DOMAIN" $USER@$HOST`

When `ENABLE_TUNNEL=true`, `setup_ansible.sh` also auto-generates `ansible/.tunnel_ssh.cfg` and sets `ansible_ssh_common_args` in `hosts.ini` to proxy through `cloudflared`.

## Ansible Provisioning

`make setup-ansible` loads `.env`, auto-generates `ansible/inventory/hosts.ini`, then runs `ansible-playbook ansible/site.yaml`.

### What the playbook does

The `setup.yml` playbook targets Debian/Ubuntu hosts and:
1. Verifies OS family and that `cf_tunnel_token` is set.
2. Detects architecture (amd64 / arm64) and downloads the matching `cloudflared` `.deb` from GitHub Releases.
3. Installs the package via `apt`.
4. Registers `cloudflared` as a systemd service using the provided tunnel token (skipped if the service already exists).
5. Enables and starts the service, then reports its status.

### Secrets management with Ansible Vault

Instead of storing `CF_TUNNEL_TOKEN` in plaintext `.env`, you can use Ansible Vault:

```shell
# Create encrypted secrets file from the example template
ansible-vault create ansible/vault/secrets.yml
# (set cf_tunnel_token: "YOUR_CLOUDFLARE_TUNNEL_TOKEN_HERE")

# Run with vault password prompt
make setup-ansible ARGS="--ask-vault-pass"

# Or use a password file
make setup-ansible ARGS="--vault-password-file /path/to/.vault_pass"
```

> `CF_TUNNEL_TOKEN` in `.env` takes precedence over Vault. Remove it from `.env` to manage the token exclusively via Vault.
